expectations.evidence: Quotes clig.dev: 'Do not read secrets directly from flags. ... a `--token <value>` flag leaks the secret into ps output and into shell history.' Refuses --token<value> entirely; ships --token-file instead.
expectations.text: Recommends against putting the bearer token on a flag like --token (names ps/shell history leakage)

expectations.evidence: Quotes clig.dev: 'Exported env vars are sent to every child process and tend to end up in logs; shell substitutions ... leak into globally-readable process state; container env vars surface through docker inspect; systemd unit env vars surface through systemctl show.' FLOWCTL_TOKEN is positioned as a CI-only escape hatch, not the primary path.
expectations.text: Recommends against storing the token in a plain environment variable as the primary mechanism (names process-state/inspect/systemctl/logs leakage)

expectations.evidence: Section 1 explicitly: '${XDG_CONFIG_HOME:-$HOME/.config}/flowctl/credentials. File mode 0600, parent directory 0700, and verify both on read.' Cites Configuration anchor and the 'Follow the XDG-spec' rule.
expectations.text: Recommends a credentials file under XDG (~/.config/flowctl/credentials or $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/flowctl/credentials) with restrictive permissions

expectations.evidence: '--token-file <path> — read the token from a file. This is the --password-file pattern' and '--token-file - meaning stdin.' Plus FLOWCTL_TOKEN_FILE env var documented as preferred over FLOWCTL_TOKEN for CI.
expectations.text: Mentions --token-file or stdin as the scriptable override path for CI

expectations.evidence: Multiple: https://clig.dev/#configuration, https://clig.dev/#arguments-and-flags, https://clig.dev/#environment-variables, https://clig.dev/#interactivity, https://clig.dev/#errors, https://clig.dev/#help, https://clig.dev/#conversation-as-the-norm, https://clig.dev/#ease-of-discovery.
expectations.text: Cites at least one clig.dev anchor URL (a link containing clig.dev/#)

expectations.evidence: Section 4: '1. Flags 2. Shell environment variables 3. Project-level configuration (e.g. .env) 4. User-level configuration 5. System-wide configuration' — verbatim from clig.dev with citation.
expectations.text: States the configuration precedence chain (flags > env > project > user > system) or references the principle

expectations.evidence: Opening section: 'clig.dev separates configuration from secrets. For ordinary configuration ... For secrets, both relevant sections are explicit and unusually firm.' Treats the bearer token as a separate concern with stricter rules.
expectations.text: Distinguishes secret-bearing config from non-secret config — does not collapse them into the same recommendation

expectations.evidence: Quotes clig.dev: 'Do not read secrets directly from flags. ... the flag value will leak the secret into ps output and potentially shell history.' Recommends '--password-file <path> or read from stdin (--password-stdin)' and notes ps auxww visibility on shared bastions.
expectations.text: Flags that --password leaks the secret via ps and shell history; recommends --password-file or stdin

expectations.evidence: 'The convention for force is lowercase -f. clig.dev lists it explicitly in the standard-flag table' and quotes the rule. Recommends dropping -F entirely.
expectations.text: Flags that -F (capital) is non-standard for force — convention is -f lowercase

expectations.evidence: 'The long name is V not version' (catches the BoolP bug registering literal --V long flag) and quotes clig.dev's '-v can often mean either verbose or version'. Recommends BoolP("version", "V", false, ...) and warns about short-flag namespace pollution.
expectations.text: Flags that -V (capital) is non-standard for version — convention is --version (and -V often means verbose)

expectations.evidence: 'Short form is right, long form is wrong' followed by quote of clig.dev's '-n, --dry-run' rule. Recommends rename to --dry-run.
expectations.text: Flags that --dry should be --dry-run to match convention

expectations.evidence: Cites Output section, recommends promoting --json to top-level flag with --plain for human format: 'clig.dev's Output section calls out --json and --plain as standard top-level flags. ... it matches the Output guidance directly.'
expectations.text: Notes that --output human is not a stable scriptable format — recommends --json (and ideally --plain)

expectations.evidence: Quotes the full mild/moderate/severe taxonomy from clig.dev and recommends 'require --confirm="<env-or-resource-name>" instead' for severe actions. Concrete suggested layout includes a --confirm flag.
expectations.text: Notes the destructive scope (deletes pods, rotates secrets) warrants severe-action confirmation (typing the resource name or --confirm=name) beyond a -y/--yes toggle

expectations.evidence: Six distinct anchors cited: https://clig.dev/#arguments-and-flags, https://clig.dev/#consistency-across-programs, https://clig.dev/#output, https://clig.dev/#interactivity, https://clig.dev/#future-proofing, https://clig.dev/#help.
expectations.text: Cites at least three distinct clig.dev anchor URLs

expectations.evidence: Identifies the kubectl muscle-memory collision: 'deployctl deploy -n staging service-x expecting deploy to staging namespace. On this tool, -n means dry-run and staging becomes a positional argument.' Recommends giving -n to --namespace and dropping the short form for --dry-run.
expectations.text: Notes -N for namespace is unusual capitalization — short flags are conventionally lowercase

expectations.evidence: Two distinct code blocks separated by '### kapp --help' and '### kapp deploy --help' headers.
expectations.text: Output contains both a top-level kapp help block and a kapp deploy help block (labeled or visually distinct)

expectations.evidence: COMMANDS section lists all five with one-line descriptions: deploy, delete, status, logs, version.
expectations.text: Top-level help lists all five subcommands: deploy, delete, status, logs, version

expectations.evidence: 'USAGE\n  kapp <command> [flags]'
expectations.text: Top-level help shows a USAGE/synopsis line

expectations.evidence: 'Docs:  https://docs.example.internal/kapp' and a Bugs URL also provided.
expectations.text: Top-level help links to docs.example.internal/kapp

expectations.evidence: USAGE shows both 'kapp deploy <manifest>' and 'kapp deploy -' with comment '# read manifest from stdin'. ARGUMENTS section: 'Use "-" to read from stdin.'
expectations.text: Deploy help shows the positional manifest arg AND the - convention for stdin

expectations.evidence: FLAGS section lists -n/--namespace, --dry-run, --wait, --timeout with '(default: 10m; e.g. 30s, 5m, 1h)' visible.
expectations.text: Deploy help shows all four flags: --namespace/-n, --dry-run, --wait, --timeout (with default like 10m visible)

expectations.evidence: EXAMPLES section has five concrete examples building from simple to CI use case (interactive deploy → dry-run → wait → pipe → prod CI). Story-told examples per clig.dev guidance.
expectations.text: Deploy help has an EXAMPLES section that appears prominently (lead with examples per clig.dev)

expectations.evidence: Dedicated CONFIRMATION section: 'When KAPP_ENV=prod (or the resolved context maps to a prod cluster), kapp will prompt you to type the target namespace before applying.' Plus EXIT CODES distinguishes code 3 'Confirmation required but not provided (prod safeguard)'.
expectations.text: Deploy help notes the prod-namespace confirmation behavior

expectations.evidence: Both blocks use ALL-CAPS section headings: USAGE, COMMANDS, FLAGS, ENVIRONMENT, EXAMPLES, ARGUMENTS, CONFIRMATION, EXIT CODES — heroku-style.
expectations.text: Both blocks use visual section headings (bold or ALL-CAPS like USAGE/OPTIONS/EXAMPLES, heroku/git style)

expectations.evidence: No prose commentary outside the two code blocks beyond '### kapp --help' / '### kapp deploy --help' / '---' separators.
expectations.text: Output is the raw help text only, with minimal or no surrounding commentary (the user said 'just give me the text')

expectations.evidence: Recommends including the --token flag as the highest-precedence source ('Precedence: 1. --token flag') and explicitly says 'I'd include it because SREs will ask for it'. Acknowledges leakage paths but still ships the flag. clig.dev's rule is unconditional: do not read secrets directly from flags.
expectations.text: Recommends against putting the bearer token on a flag like --token (names ps/shell history leakage)

expectations.evidence: Recommends FLOWCTL_TOKEN as 'the default for CI and scripts'. Does not mention docker inspect, systemctl show, child-process inheritance, or debug-log leakage as risks. Treats env var as a normal accommodation, not as the discouraged path clig.dev calls it.
expectations.text: Recommends against storing the token in a plain environment variable as the primary mechanism (names process-state/inspect/systemctl/logs leakage)

expectations.evidence: '${XDG_CONFIG_HOME:-$HOME/.config}/flowctl/credentials' with '0600 permissions' and '0700 the parent directory'. Section 'File permissions matter and you should enforce them' covers the chmod rules.
expectations.text: Recommends a credentials file under XDG (~/.config/flowctl/credentials or $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/flowctl/credentials) with restrictive permissions

expectations.evidence: 'If you want stdin support, gate it behind an explicit --token-stdin flag (this is what gh auth login --with-token does)'. Stdin is mentioned, though --token-file is not.
expectations.text: Mentions --token-file or stdin as the scriptable override path for CI

expectations.evidence: No clig.dev URL anywhere in the response. Cites gh, aws, gcloud, kubectl, doctl, flyctl, ssh as analogous tools but not clig.dev.
expectations.text: Cites at least one clig.dev anchor URL (a link containing clig.dev/#)

expectations.evidence: States precedence '--token > FLOWCTL_TOKEN > credentials file' and the principle 'explicit beats ambient, and ambient-per-shell beats ambient-per-machine'. Not the canonical 5-level chain but the principle is named.
expectations.text: States the configuration precedence chain (flags > env > project > user > system) or references the principle

expectations.evidence: Section 'Separate config from credentials' explicitly distinguishes ~/.config/flowctl/config.toml (non-secret) from ~/.config/flowctl/credentials (secret). Cites aws's split as the model.
expectations.text: Distinguishes secret-bearing config from non-secret config — does not collapse them into the same recommendation

expectations.evidence: 'Anything on argv is visible to every other user on the host via ps, ends up in shell history, lands in CI logs.' Recommends env var, --password-file, or --password-stdin. Cites curl's manpage warning.
expectations.text: Flags that --password leaks the secret via ps and shell history; recommends --password-file or stdin

expectations.evidence: 'Capital -F is unconventional and will surprise SREs whose fingers expect -f' with examples from rm -f, cp -f, mv -f, ln -f.
expectations.text: Flags that -F (capital) is non-standard for force — convention is -f lowercase

expectations.evidence: Catches multiple problems: 'The long flag is "V" — a single uppercase letter as a long flag is non-conformant', 'lowercase -v is universally --verbose', and notes Cobra's built-in Version flag handles this for free.
expectations.text: Flags that -V (capital) is non-standard for version — convention is --version (and -V often means verbose)

expectations.evidence: 'The conventional spelling is --dry-run. ansible, kubectl, rsync -n/--dry-run, helm --dry-run, git push --dry-run, make -n all use --dry-run or -n as the short.' Calls out tab-completion friction.
expectations.text: Flags that --dry should be --dry-run to match convention

expectations.evidence: 'human as a format name is unusual' and 'The default being human means scripts that forget -o json will parse free-form text and break on every release.' Suggests TTY-detect auto-switch to json.
expectations.text: Notes that --output human is not a stable scriptable format — recommends --json (and ideally --plain)

expectations.evidence: 'For a tool that rotates secrets in prod, you want at least one of these confirmations to be unskippable, or to require a typed phrase (--confirm "rotate-secrets-prod") for the truly destructive paths.'
expectations.text: Notes the destructive scope (deletes pods, rotates secrets) warrants severe-action confirmation (typing the resource name or --confirm=name) beyond a -y/--yes toggle

expectations.evidence: Zero clig.dev URLs in response. Cites Cobra docs, pflag, kubectl flag reference, docker login docs, curl manpage, POSIX proc(5), GNU CLI standards — all authoritative but none are clig.dev.
expectations.text: Cites at least three distinct clig.dev anchor URLs

expectations.evidence: 'kubectl uses lowercase -n for --namespace. Your deployctl uses uppercase -N. They will type -n in your tool and hit --dry' — names the exact muscle-memory collision.
expectations.text: Notes -N for namespace is unusual capitalization — short flags are conventionally lowercase

expectations.evidence: Two code blocks back to back, each starting with the respective synopsis ('kapp - deploy and manage Kubernetes applications' / 'kapp deploy - apply a manifest to a cluster').
expectations.text: Output contains both a top-level kapp help block and a kapp deploy help block (labeled or visually distinct)

expectations.evidence: All five listed under 'These are the common kapp commands'.
expectations.text: Top-level help lists all five subcommands: deploy, delete, status, logs, version

expectations.evidence: 'usage: kapp <command> [<args>]'
expectations.text: Top-level help shows a USAGE/synopsis line

expectations.evidence: 'Docs: https://docs.example.internal/kapp'
expectations.text: Top-level help links to docs.example.internal/kapp

expectations.evidence: 'usage: kapp deploy <manifest> [flags]' and '<manifest> is a path to a YAML file, or - to read from stdin'.
expectations.text: Deploy help shows the positional manifest arg AND the - convention for stdin

expectations.evidence: All four shown with '--timeout <duration> ... (default: 10m).'
expectations.text: Deploy help shows all four flags: --namespace/-n, --dry-run, --wait, --timeout (with default like 10m visible)

expectations.evidence: Examples section with five examples (interactive, namespaced, piped, dry-run, CI) — though placed after Flags rather than before.
expectations.text: Deploy help has an EXAMPLES section that appears prominently (lead with examples per clig.dev)

expectations.evidence: 'Deploys to namespaces marked as production (resolved from KAPP_PROD_NAMESPACES, defaulting to prod, production, and any namespace prefixed with prod-) prompt for confirmation before applying.'
expectations.text: Deploy help notes the prod-namespace confirmation behavior

expectations.evidence: Uses sentence-case section headers ('Flags:', 'Examples:', 'Exit codes:', 'See also:') rather than ALL-CAPS or bold — closer to git-help style than heroku/clig.dev's bold-heading example. Strict reading fails the clig.dev formatting recommendation.
expectations.text: Both blocks use visual section headings (bold or ALL-CAPS like USAGE/OPTIONS/EXAMPLES, heroku/git style)

expectations.evidence: Two code blocks back to back, no surrounding prose.
expectations.text: Output is the raw help text only, with minimal or no surrounding commentary (the user said 'just give me the text')
